_comment: DRAFT — Shoulder rehabilitation exercises. Not yet clinically reviewed. Do not integrate into runtime code.
_condition: Shoulder rehabilitation — rotator cuff, impingement, post-surgical

exercises.name: Pendulum Shoulder
exercises.description: Lean forward with the unaffected arm supported on a table. Allow the affected arm to hang freely and use gentle body weight shifts to create small circular or figure-eight movements. A gravity-assisted distraction exercise for the early post-operative shoulder.
exercises.tempo: Gentle pendulum motion — let gravity do the work
exercises.cue: Your arm should feel heavy and relaxed. Do not use the shoulder muscles to swing — move your body to initiate the motion.
exercises.painNote: Mild traction sensation is normal. Stop if you feel sharp pain or catching in the joint.
exercises.imageAlt: Person leaning forward with one arm hanging freely, making small gentle circles
exercises.progressionNotes: Increase circle size as pain allows. Progress to active-assisted ROM exercises.

exercises.name: Shoulder External Rotation — Sidelying
exercises.description: Lying on your side with the affected arm uppermost, elbow bent to 90° and a folded towel under the arm. Rotate the forearm upward (externally) keeping the elbow at the side. Targets infraspinatus and teres minor — key rotator cuff muscles.
exercises.cue: Keep the elbow tucked into your side. The movement comes from the shoulder, not the wrist.
exercises.painNote: Work in a pain-free range only. Pinching in the shoulder at end range — reduce the arc.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying on side with elbow bent, rotating the forearm upward
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to standing ER with resistance band.

exercises.name: Shoulder Internal Rotation — Resistance Band
exercises.description: Stand beside a door or anchor. With the elbow at 90° and tucked to the side, pull the band across the body (internal rotation). Strengthens subscapularis, the largest rotator cuff muscle.
exercises.cue: Keep the elbow pinned to your side throughout. Only the forearm should move.
exercises.painNote: If you feel shoulder impingement (catching at the front of the shoulder), check that the elbow is not drifting forward.
exercises.imageAlt: Person standing with resistance band, pulling forearm across body with elbow at side
exercises.progressionNotes: Increase band resistance. Progress to 90° abduction IR/ER for sport-specific loading.

exercises.name: Scapular Retraction
exercises.description: Sitting or standing, squeeze your shoulder blades together as if trying to hold a pencil between them, hold briefly, then release. Activates the middle trapezius and rhomboids — essential for restoring normal scapular mechanics.
exercises.cue: Think about your shoulder blades moving toward the spine and slightly downward. Do not shrug your shoulders up.
exercises.imageAlt: Person standing with arms at sides, visibly squeezing shoulder blades together
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to prone Y-T-W exercises once scapular control is established.

exercises.name: Wall Slide
exercises.description: Stand facing a wall with forearms resting on the wall at shoulder height. Slide both arms upward overhead while maintaining contact with the wall and keeping the core braced. Improves shoulder flexion range and trains serratus anterior.
exercises.cue: Keep your lower back flat — do not let it arch as your arms go overhead. Push lightly into the wall throughout.
exercises.painNote: Go only as high as you can without pain or compensatory trunk extension.
exercises.imageAlt: Person with forearms on a wall, sliding arms upward overhead while maintaining wall contact
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to overhead pressing once full wall slide ROM is pain-free.

exercises.name: Prone Y Raise
exercises.description: Lying face down on a bench or mat, raise both arms in a Y shape (approximately 120°) with thumbs pointing up. Targets lower trapezius and serratus anterior — critical for scapular upward rotation and shoulder stability.
exercises.cue: Lead with your thumbs — rotate slightly outward as you raise. Do not shrug your shoulders toward your ears.
exercises.painNote: Use very light resistance initially. This exercise is harder than it looks.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying face down, lifting both arms in a Y shape with thumbs up
exercises.progressionNotes: Progress to T raise and W raise to form the full Y-T-W complex.

exercises.name: Prone T Raise
exercises.description: Lying face down, raise both arms out to the sides in a T shape (90° abduction) with thumbs pointing up. Targets middle trapezius. Often paired with Y raise and W raise as the Y-T-W complex.
exercises.cue: Squeeze your shoulder blades together as you lift. Thumbs point to the ceiling.
exercises.painNote: Stop for any pain at the top of the shoulder (impingement position). Check external rotation is maintained.
exercises.imageAlt: Person lying face down lifting arms out to the sides in a T shape
exercises.progressionNotes: Add light dumbbells (1–2 kg) once 3×12 is comfortable.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart
exercises.description: Hold a resistance band with both hands at shoulder height and arms straight. Pull the band apart by moving both hands outward until the band touches the chest. Strengthens posterior shoulder, middle trap, and rear deltoids — important for posture and shoulder health.
exercises.cue: Keep your arms straight and at shoulder height throughout. Squeeze the shoulder blades together as the band comes to your chest.
exercises.painNote: If you feel shoulder pain at end range, reduce band tension or shorten the movement arc.
exercises.imageAlt: Person holding a resistance band at shoulder height, pulling hands apart until band touches chest
exercises.progressionNotes: Increase band resistance. Can be done with 90° elbow bend for variation.
